data.title: Taipei mayor cuts Shanghai visit short to oversee security following deadly stabbing
data.description:
Taipei Mayor Chiang Wan-an has cut short his planned visit to a joint forum in Shanghai to better focus on security concerns following a deadly stabbing incident in the city last week.
Originally scheduled to attend the Shanghai-Taipei City Forum on Saturday and Sunday, Chiang will now only take part in the main forum on Sunday morning before returning to Taipei to oversee heightened security operations, according to a press release from the municipal government on Tuesday.
The Shanghai-Taipei City Forum has been held annually since 2010 and remains one of the few events attended by officials from both sides of the Taiwan Strait.
Chiang said that ensuring the safety of citizens remained the “top priority”.
“To ensure stability in Taipei and reassure the public, while also balancing the ongoing municipal exchanges through the Taipei-Shanghai City Forum, which has been held for 15 years, he has decided to shorten his personal trip,” the notice said.
Speaking to the press on Wednesday, Chiang reaffirmed that the core spirit of the joint forum was “good cities, good cross-strait relations”, with the goal of continuing municipal exchanges between Taipei and Shanghai to achieve tangible outcomes in cooperation.


The decision comes amid escalating concerns over public safety as Taiwan prepares for the holiday season, which typically sees large gatherings and festivities.
The violent attack at Taipei Main Station on Friday, which resulted in four deaths, including the attacker, and 11 injuries, has heightened alert levels across the city.
The suspect, identified as 27-year-old Chang Wen, carried out his rampage using knives and smoke devices in crowded public areas before falling from a building and dying. Authorities are investigating the motives behind the violence.
According to the Tuesday press release, Chiang has demanded that the entire city elevate security levels across all areas.
Taipei has strengthened its security measures, including emergency drills at major transport hubs. Authorities have also formed specialised anti-terrorism teams and are exploring artificial intelligence-based threat detection to enhance real-time safety and rapid response capabilities.
Chiang has personally presided over the emergency security response team meetings for four consecutive days, closely monitoring the progress of the investigation, the strengthening of security at public venues and major events, as well as the steps taken to comfort the injured and soothe public concerns.
Despite the reduced attendance, Taipei’s delegation to Shanghai, led by deputy mayor Lin Yi-hua, will still take part in the full discussions aimed at improving municipal collaboration.
On Wednesday, Lin told local media that because of the stabbing incident in Taipei, it was important for the mayor to maintain stability. She also explained that after 15 years of holding the forum, both Taipei and Shanghai had worked hard to understand each other better.
The forum’s theme this year is “technology transforming life”, focusing on urban innovation and cross-strait cooperation.
According to Taiwan’s United Daily News, the forum was initially planned for September but was delayed as Taiwan’s government needed more time to review the memorandums of understanding (MOUs) expected to be signed at the event.
Taiwanese media also reported that during the forum, the two cities were expected to formalise agreements on water management and vocational training.
Earlier, Chiang told the Taipei city council his Shanghai itinerary would “not involve politics”.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Beijing’s Taiwan Affairs Office spokesman, Chen Binhua, said Taipei and Shanghai had maintained communication regarding the organisation of the forum, with Beijing supporting cross-strait exchanges across various fields.
Shanghai’s vice-mayor Hua Yuan visited Taipei for the forum last year, marking the first senior city official visit in six years.
The Taipei mayor has typically attended the forum in both cities, including former mayors Ko Wen-je and Hau Lung-pin.

data.title: Zheng Qinwen to join Osaka, Alcaraz in 1 Point Slam before Australian Open
data.description:
China’s Zheng Qinwen will resume her comeback at an exhibition event with a difference on the eve of next month’s Australian Open: a tournament consisting of one-point matches.
Zheng, who had surgery on a troublesome right elbow in July and has barely played since, will join the likes of Naomi Osaka, Carlos Alcaraz and Jannik Sinner in the AO 1 Point Slam at Melbourne’s Rod Laver Arena.
Ten amateur players will try their luck against 22 professionals in knockout rounds, with “rock, paper, scissors” deciding who serves in each sudden-death point. The winner will take home A$1 million (US$671,000) – only slightly less than the A$1.1 million earned by singles semi-finalists at the last Australian Open proper.
The day before the 1 Point Slam on January 14, Olympic champion Zheng will play an exhibition match against Elena Rybakina as she gears up for the Australian Open itself, which will be held from January 18 to February 1.

Melbourne will see the latest attempt at a return to action for Zheng, who underwent elbow surgery after Wimbledon, missing over two months of competition and skipping the US Open.
She returned at the China Open two months later but retired in the third set of her third-round match, citing the injury, and withdrew from the Wuhan and Ningbo Opens.
At last month’s National Games, Zheng served as flag-bearer for her home province of Hubei, before withdrawing from the tournament.
“I need to extend my recovery period … and only resume extensive playing training once I’m fully recovered,” Zheng said in an interview with Vogue China this month.
She said she had made progress and her recovery was “at 95 out of 100”.
Zheng’s world ranking has dropped from a career-high No 4 in August 2024 to No 24.
Before her surgery, she defeated top seed Aryna Sabalenka in straight sets at the Italian Open – her first win over the world No 1 in seven meetings – then reached the quarter-finals of the French Open, her best result there.

data.title: US to grant South Korea exception on nuclear submarine fuel supply
data.description:
South Korea and the US have agreed to pursue a separate agreement to formalise Seoul’s right to build nuclear-powered submarines, and working-level talks will begin early next year, the Asian country’s national security adviser said on Wednesday.
Speaking to reporters after visiting Washington, Wi Sung-lac said he discussed the issue and other security arrangements with senior US officials including Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Energy Secretary Chris Wright.
His trip was aimed at accelerating the implementation of commitments outlined in a joint fact sheet released after a summit between President Lee Jae-myung and US President Donald Trump in October, including cooperation on enriched uranium, spent fuel reprocessing and nuclear-powered submarines.

“We agreed that a separate agreement is necessary for nuclear submarine cooperation and decided to pursue it,” Wi said at a briefing.
The new pact would be treated as an exception, which would require approval by Trump and US Congress.
South Korea is seeking to equip submarines with a reactor using low-enriched fuel at levels of 20 per cent or less, and has no plans to adopt highly enriched uranium, he said, adding that he had highlighted Lee’s commitment to non-proliferation.
A working-level US delegation is likely to visit Seoul early next year to follow up on the agreements listed in the joint fact sheet, and both sides will set some milestones for performance reviews later next year, Wi said.

South Korea is largely banned from enriching uranium under a civilian nuclear energy deal with the US. Wi has said Seoul was looking at Australia’s case where it had obtained an exemption through a stand-alone accord with Washington, allowing Canberra to sign an Aukus accord with the US and UK in 2021.
Seoul’s defence minister has said he aims to conclude negotiations with the US within two years on securing an arrangement for the supply of nuclear fuel.
Wi also said he and his US counterparts explored ways to revive talks with North Korea possibly around the first half of next year, though he wasn’t targeting specific diplomatic occasions.
“We are considering various opportunities and won’t rule out any in order to engage with North Korea. I won’t go into specifics, but if there are any opportunities next year, we would try to utilise them,” he said.
Additional reporting by The Korea Times

data.title: Thai marines rescue starving lions, bears from Cambodian border casino
data.description:
Thai marines have rescued five starving animals – two lions and three bears – abandoned in cages at a bombed-out Cambodian casino complex amid a deepening border fight between the neighbours.
Security forces were conducting a sweep of the seized building on Sunday in Trat province when they made the grim discovery, prompting them to send a veterinary team to the site the following day.
Officials from the Department of National Parks, Wildlife and Plant Conservation (DNP) evaluated the condition of a pair of lions, two Asiatic black bears and one sun bear, describing them as scrawny due to chronic underfeeding and prolonged neglect at Thmor Dar Casino that Thailand struck earlier this month.
DNP chief Atthapol Charoenchansa said the animals’ ribs were visible and tranquillising them posed a high risk of heart failure.
Atthapol said veterinarians administered vitamins to the animals as soldiers and park officials raced against time to move the cages out of the conflict-torn area.
The bears were eventually transported to the Bang Lamung Wildlife Breeding Station in Chon Buri where they are recovering, while the lions found a new home at the Khao Son Wildlife Breeding Station in Ratchaburi.
Officials said they were collecting evidence and verifying the animals’ origins to take legal action under Thai wildlife conservation laws, The Nation Thailand reported.

Some Thai social media users commended the rescue operation and hoped the animals quickly recovered.
“I feel sorry for the little ones. Thank you to all officers,” a user said.
The wildlife relocation comes as Bangkok and Phnom Penh are set to hold four days of talks that begin on Wednesday in Thailand’s Chanthaburi province to find ways to end the violence that has killed dozens and displaced more than half a million people on both sides of their 800km (500-mile) border since renewed fighting erupted earlier this month.
In July, five days of fighting ended with a ceasefire deal brokered by Malaysia and US President Donald Trump.
The Thai military is expected to raise five issues during the discussions, which are likely to conclude with a meeting of the two countries’ defence ministers on Saturday.
Thailand’s ministry of defence also outlined several demands to be discussed ahead of the bilateral meeting of the General Border Committee (GBC) on Saturday.
The defence ministry said the Thai side would not proceed with the GBC meeting or sign any agreement on Saturday should secretariat-level discussions fail to reach agreement on key technical frameworks such as troop deployments.
Cambodia earlier asked that the meetings be held in Kuala Lumpur instead, but Thailand rejected the request.
The two sides normally take turns hosting the bilateral gatherings and the last GBC meeting was held in September in Cambodia’s Koh Kong province.
Additional reporting by Bloomberg

data.title: Hong Kong customs seize counterfeit goods worth HK$36 million in crackdown
data.description:
Hong Kong customs has intercepted more than 83,000 counterfeit items worth about HK$36 million (US$4.62 million) as part of a recent crackdown, warning that criminals are using the heightened seasonal demand as a “smokescreen” to move illicit goods to Europe and the Americas.
The Customs and Excise Department said on Wednesday that the operation ran from December 8 to 19 and was conducted in response to a surge in cross-border logistics activities during the peak year-end shopping season.
During the operation, authorities uncovered 28 cases involving a diverse range of fake luxury products meant for “relatively high-spending markets” around the world, customs said.
“Criminals are exploiting the massive year-end logistics peak as a ‘smokescreen’ to move illicit goods, hoping the sheer volume of holiday traffic will help them evade detection,” Senior Investigation Officer Lin Kwan-yiu of the department said.
“In this operation, we detected a total of 28 cases and seized approximately 83,000 items of suspected counterfeit goods, with an estimated market value of about HK$36 million.”
He added that syndicates were increasingly using high-quality counterfeits that mimicked authentic brands.
“We believe these criminals are attempting to mix these high-quality fakes with genuine products for sale during the Christmas period,” Lin said.
“By blurring the lines between real and fake, they aim to secure higher profit margins from unsuspecting consumers.”
Some goods were intended for destinations such as the Middle East and Africa, with customs saying they believed the items would be repackaged in those locations and then exported to premium markets to lower operational costs and evade detection.
Lin said that authorities for the first time had noticed an increase in suspicious shipments heading to South America and Central Africa, suggesting counterfeiters were aggressively seeking “new markets” during the festive season.
Most of the seizures were the result of cooperation with Hong Kong’s logistics industry, with 27 of the cases uncovered at transshipment centres, he said.
The 28th case was intercepted on the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge, where customs officers stopped an inbound truck and arrested the driver.
The suspect, a 55-year-old man, has been released on bail pending further investigation.
Lin said that with the holiday rush in full swing, the logistics industry had been urged to remain vigilant about its clients’ cargo.
Consumers were also warned to stick to reputable retailers.
“Under the Trade Descriptions Ordinance, any person who imports, exports or possesses counterfeit goods for the purpose of trade commits an offence,” Lin said.
The maximum penalty for such crimes was a HK$500,000 fine and five years in prison, he added.
Members of the public are encouraged to report suspicious activities via customs’ 24-hour hotline at 182-8080 or through their official crime-reporting email.

data.title: China actress Mao Xiaotong abandoned by dad, rejects his cash demands after becoming famous
data.description:
Award-winning Chinese actress Mao Xiaotong may be famous for her sweet and cute appearance, but she has a tough streak that has seen her walk away from toxic relationships with her father and ex-boyfriend.
Mao, 37, has won multiple awards including the Magnolia Award for Best Supporting Actress and the Huading Award for Best Actress in Contemporary Drama.
She is widely recognised for playing independent and innocent female characters in Chinese television dramas, such as Nothing but Thirty and Light Beyond the Reed.

Her look is so famously pure that some compared her with memes featuring cute kittens.
In contrast, she has decisive characteristics that helped her break up toxic relationships.
Mao was born prematurely in 1988 in northern China’s Tianjin municipality, when her mother accidentally stretched her belly while running a stall during pregnancy.

Mao’s father, an alcoholic gambler, treated her badly because she was not a boy.
Her mother divorced her father when she was two years old, changed her surname to her own and raised Mao alone.
Mao’s mother worked on several part-time jobs to support her dream of pursuing the arts.
She had already appeared on stage by the age of four and began studying Latin dance in Beijing at 13.
At 17, she decided to study performing arts at the Central Academy of Drama and was admitted after one attempt in 2005.

Mao made her first major career breakthrough for a role in the hit 2011 drama Empresses in the Palace.
In 2018, her father, who disappeared from her life when she was two, appeared on a television show and demanded 50 million yuan (US$7 million) from his “big star daughter”.
He also sought a villa and car like Mao gave her mother.
Mao never openly responded to her father. Online observers supported her, encouraging her to not succumb to blackmail.
Around the same time, Mao also openly broke up with her boyfriend, singer and actor Chen Xiang, after she found him with another woman at their home.
Chen’s fans attacked Mao after seeing Chen acting frustrated following their break-up. But Mao fought back by publishing the surveillance footage from the lift of their building.
The footage showed Chen entering the lift with an actress just before Mao came home.

It also showed that it only took Mao three hours to pack her things and leave, ending a relationship that had lasted for years.
Many praised Mao’s decisiveness in leaving the toxic relationship.
Mao once said in an interview: “Never underestimate a woman’s courage to solve problems.”
She said she learned her independent spirit from her mother, whom she considers to be an “iron lady”.
Mao’s social media account now has 22 million followers.
“She is the most beautiful, strong yet tragic Chinese actress I know. I can see a great girl’s power in her,” said one online observer.

data.title: Helper who survived Hong Kong fire recovers in Philippines for Christmas
data.description:
The Filipino domestic worker who fought for her life to escape from Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades with her employer’s three-month-old baby is spending Christmas with her family back home after being treated for pneumonia.
While 28-year-old Rhodora Alcaraz's time in intensive care is long behind her, she is still recovering from pneumonia and has been referred to a psychiatrist to help process her trauma.
The helper was earlier lauded by Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr as a hero for her role in helping save a baby girl during last month’s fire at Wang Fuk Court.
She returned to her hometown in San Pablo in southern Luzon, just southeast of Manila, in the company of her sisters last Tuesday.
The city of San Pablo is also home to Alcaraz’s six-year-old son and her partner, as well as her parents and siblings.
Cynthia Abdon-Tellez, general manager of the Mission for Migrant Workers, said Alcaraz had been fine when she arrived at Ninoy Aquino International Airport last week, but began feeling weak again on Saturday.
The charity manager, who has been communicating with Alcaraz via text, said the helper was still reliant on a wheelchair to get around and could not bathe or use the toilet without help.

Abdon-Tellez said the helper was at one point taken to the Overseas Filipino Workers Hospital and Diagnostic Centre in Pampanga by ambulance, and spent four days there being treated for low potassium levels and pneumonia. She was discharged on Tuesday evening.
“I decided to go home since I felt a bit better, I can walk by myself and also because I cannot get enough rest or sleep in hospital. The doctor let me go home,” Alcaraz said in a text message seen by the Post.
“I was told to go back for follow-up checks because my CT scan showed some findings. I was told by the doctor that I had developed pneumonia and that I needed to see a psychiatrist because I had trauma and anxiety.”
A spokesman for the Philippines’ consulate in Hong Kong on Wednesday confirmed that Alcaraz was discharged from hospital in the country the day before, with a follow-up health check scheduled for January.
Romulo Victor M Israel Jnr, the country’s top envoy in Hong Kong, told the Post last week that Alcaraz’s injuries were “more internal”, affecting her respiratory system.
She inhaled carbon monoxide during the fire and was placed in intensive care at Hong Kong’s United Christian Hospital to have soot removed from her lungs.
Alcaraz was the only Filipino helper to suffer life-threatening injuries in the Tai Po fire. Another domestic worker from the country and nine Indonesians died in the blaze.
Last month’s fire engulfed seven of Wang Fuk Court’s eight high-rise towers, lasting for 43 hours and claiming 161 lives.

data.title: Top PLA Air Force duo miss key military event led by Xi as corruption fight rolls on
data.description:
Two top PLA Air Force officers have become the latest military brass to miss a key political event, as the anti-corruption campaign in the Chinese military continues unabated.
Footage broadcast by state television CCTV indicated that General Chang Dingqiu and Guo Puxiao, Air Force commander and political commissar, respectively, were not present at a ceremony on Monday to fill top-level vacancies caused by the sweeping crackdown.
The event in Beijing was led by President Xi Jinping, who is also chairman of the Central Military Commission (CMC), the top command body for China’s armed forces.

Chang, 58, has long been viewed as a rising military star on a fast track to promotion. He was for many years the youngest officer at his rank, including when he was promoted to Air Force commander in August 2021 at age 54.
Chang’s profile on the Chinese search engine Baidu has been inaccessible since last week.
Beijing has made no statement on Chang’s status. His last public appearance was in October, in official coverage of the fourth plenary session for the sitting Central Committee of the Communist Party. He attended as a full member of the committee, which is China’s top policymaking body.
The absence of Chang recalls those of other PLA generals, including the commanders of the PLA ground force and the navy, both of whom have not been seen in public for months and also missed October’s party conclave.
Ground force commander Li Qiaoming and navy commander Hu Zhongming were among the 17 military officers absent from the fourth plenum, according to CCTV footage. The duo remain full members of the Central Committee and have not been publicly targeted by the anti-corruption watchdog.
Monday’s ceremony was the first of its kind all year, signalling that the military command was starting to fill vacancies left by the massive corruption crackdown, which had seen dozens of generals abruptly removed.


Yang Zhibin and Han Shengyan, commander and political commissar of the Eastern and Central Theatre Command, respectively, were promoted at the ceremony by Xi to the rank of full general, the highest active-duty rank in the PLA.
Senior leadership promotions have slowed markedly, with the CMC holding four such ceremonies in 2023 and only three in 2024. All top generals in active duty are expected to attend.
Multiple senior PLA officers have been sacked over the past two years under an intensified anti-corruption campaign. Among the most high-profile dismissals was that of He Weidong, a former CMC vice-chairman and member of the 24-man Politburo – the party’s top decision-making body.
Other top generals to have fallen from grace include Miao Hua, once the PLA’s ideological and personnel chief, and two former defence ministers – Wei Fenghe and his successor Li Shangfu – both placed under investigation in 2023.

data.title: Philippine ex-lawmaker accused of building 5-level basement to store money
data.description:
A Philippine cabinet minister has claimed that fugitive former lawmaker Zaldy Co intends to build a five-storey basement within a luxury home to store money, as more revelations of the widening flood-control corruption scandal surface.
Interior Secretary Jonvic Remulla made the claim at a press conference on Tuesday, saying government checks of applications to a private village association and building permits suggested the Forbes Park property in Makati – one of Metro Manila’s most exclusive neighbourhoods – was being built with a basement far deeper than one built for residential parking.
“Yes, our initial investigation showed that the house being constructed by Zaldy Co has a five-storey basement,” Remulla told reporters as quoted by The Philippine Daily Inquirer.
Remulla argued that while basements in high-end homes were often designed for vehicles, a multilevel basement could also serve as secure storage. He said: “We always assume that it is for parking, but it is to our knowledge that it was going to be used for storage of money.”
He added that investigators had also determined the rationale for such a building design. “Because fire goes upwards so they put the money below in the lowest point of the house so that it won’t be harmed if there is a fire.”
Co’s camp swiftly rejected Remulla’s comments, with his lawyer Ruy Rondain issuing a statement calling the “cash basement” claim a “fabrication”. Rondain also challenged Remulla to disclose his source, saying “neither the existing building nor the construction plans support his claim”.
The home design involved a “split-type basement, strictly for vehicles”, and the layout was in line with local rules that limit a property’s buildable area relative to its total lot size, according to Rondain.

The clash comes as Philippine authorities intensify efforts to locate and arrest Co, a former congressman and businessman tied to Sunwest Construction and Development Corporation, after anti-corruption prosecutors accused him and others of defrauding the state through “ghost” and substandard flood-control projects.
In one of the most prominent cases so far, the Office of the Ombudsman filed corruption complaints at an anti-corruption court in November over alleged anomalies in a 289 million peso (US$4.9 million) flood-control project in Oriental Mindoro province, naming Co, public works officials and company executives as accused individuals.
Assistant Ombudsman Mico Clavano said the filing was being pursued “firmly, independently and without fear or favour”, adding that prosecutors were building more cases.
The court, known as the Sandiganbayan, has issued arrest warrants in the case. It later declared Co a “fugitive from justice” after prosecutors argued he had not complied with court orders and subpoenas, according to Philippine media reports.
The whereabouts of Co, who left the Philippines in July, remain unknown. President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr announced on December 10 that the government had cancelled Co’s Philippine passport and instructed the foreign ministry and police to work with embassies to expedite his return.
Authorities have raised the possibility that Co could be travelling on a foreign passport, which would complicate efforts to bring him back to the Philippines through normal immigration channels.

Remulla has told local media that Co is believed to be hiding in Portugal and may have obtained the relevant Portuguese documentation years earlier, prompting the interior department to liaise with its Portuguese counterpart on the matter.
Manila has also said it is open to using international cooperation mechanisms under the United Nations Convention Against Corruption to locate Co.
Remulla has tied the hunt for Co to a push to seize assets that authorities allege were derived from corruption, saying government lawyers were exploring civil asset forfeiture – a legal mechanism that allows the state to seize property suspected of being linked to unlawful gains even without a criminal conviction.
In an interview published Friday on the Department of the Interior’s website, Remulla said the state was examining assets it believed were connected to Co and his associates, naming “two houses in Forbes Park” as well as interests linked to a hotel and a resort. He also warned suspects under investigation that “there will be civil forfeiture of all their assets”.
The flood-control controversy has become a politically explosive issue because it touches on a vital government function in a country that has been repeatedly struck by powerful typhoons and other extreme weather events.
This year alone, the Philippines has endured dozens of tropical cyclones, including Ragasa in September and Kalmaegi and Fung-wong in November, which brought widespread flooding and forced mass evacuations.


Public anger over the corruption scandal has led to large protest rallies in Manila and other cities, drawing tens of thousands of Filipinos demanding accountability.
The government’s independent commission is reportedly reviewing thousands of flood-control projects worth hundreds of billions of pesos, amid official estimates that enormous sums may have been lost to corruption since 2023.
Marcos has sought to frame the crackdown as a test of state authority, warning that suspects would be arrested and “nobody would be spared”.

data.title: India’s new law to fuel energy needs, drive nuclear power growth
data.description:
India has taken a decisive step to open its tightly controlled nuclear power sector to private and foreign capital, signalling a shift in how the country plans to meet soaring electricity demand while cutting emissions.
Parliament earlier this month approved the Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India (Shanti) Bill, one of the most consequential overhauls of India’s civilian nuclear framework in decades, ending years of near-exclusive state control.
Under the new law, private Indian and foreign companies will be allowed to build, own and operate nuclear power plants under government licence, while long-standing liability rules that had deterred investors have been eased, according to experts.
The legislation also redefines responsibility in the event of an accident, removing liability for equipment suppliers and introducing a graded liability cap for plant operators, aligning India more closely with international nuclear norms.
Experts said the changes could unlock stalled investment and accelerate India’s ambition to raise nuclear capacity to 100 gigawatts by 2047, from about 8.8GW currently, by reducing construction delays and legal uncertainty for developers.
“The recent push for private, including foreign, funding in the nuclear sector could push India to generate 10–12 per cent of its energy needs [100GW] from modest-sized nuclear reactors,” said Uday Chandra, an independent analyst based in New Delhi.
“This is comparable to Chinese ambitions in this regard but much lower than, say, the US and France.”

Nuclear power currently contributes about 3 per cent of India’s energy mix, making it the fifth-largest source after coal, hydropower, solar and wind.
India’s renewed push mirrors a broader global trend, as nuclear plants are increasingly seen as a way to meet baseload demand – the minimum level of electricity a grid needs – because their output is far more consistent than intermittent sources such as solar and wind.
Advocates argue that nuclear energy’s ability to provide steady power with relatively low greenhouse gas emissions makes it a critical part of India’s long-term energy strategy, despite enduring concerns over safety and the disposal of nuclear waste.
Confidence has also been boosted by newer technologies such as small modular reactors, which are far smaller and more adaptable than traditional large reactors and are promoted as safer and more affordable.
Currently, about 90 per cent of India’s baseload energy demand is met through fossil fuels and large hydro-based generation.
Uttamarani Pati, analyst in renewables and power research at Rystad Energy, said nuclear power would primarily help address baseload demand, while fossil fuels would still be required to meet peak load demands given India’s growing electricity needs.
“The government is encouraging private sector investment to ease financial pressure and introduce competition, which could accelerate the pace of nuclear project development,” she said.
According to Pati, India relies primarily on its indigenous nuclear technology and growing geopolitical tensions could further isolate it, with many countries hesitant to fully engage in nuclear cooperation.
While India was pursuing advanced nuclear reactor technologies, such as fast breeder reactors and thorium reactors, to enhance sustainability and safety, Pati noted that progress had been slow due to technological hurdles, funding constraints and research limitations.

Still, the law has faced political pushback.
Nilanjan Mukhopadhay, an independent political commentator, noted that the omnibus law was introduced in parliament without really taking opposition parties in confidence and was passed despite opposition demands for referral to a parliamentary panel.
There was also opposition to privatisation of nuclear energy development, he said.
While the legislation had earned praise for potentially advancing nuclear energy development, “I am sure that it is going to be challenged”, Mukhopadhyay said.
“However, the dice is loaded in favour of what the government wants to do.”
Anjal Prakash, research director at the Indian School of Business’ Bharti Institute of Public Policy, said nuclear expansion would support India’s energy independence by reducing fossil fuel imports.
It would also leverage domestic fuel sources such as thorium, which could be found in coastal states like Kerala, Tamil Nadu and Odisha, he added.
According to Prakash, the country’s regulatory infrastructure maintains strict protocols involving quarterly inspections by authorities and ensuring low radiation emissions tougher than global benchmarks.
High upfront costs and construction delays had historically hampered the sector’s development in the country, he noted.
Public protests due to safety concerns have significantly hampered the development of nuclear power plants in India, often causing projects to fall years behind schedule and exceed initial budget estimates.
Such concerns could remain a hurdle for future projects as well, especially in a densely populated country like India, with memories of Japan’s Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster in 2011 still fresh in the region, Prakash said.


Fifteen years on, however, Japan is set to restart operations at the world’s biggest nuclear plant. A regional vote on Monday cleared the way for restarting the Kashiwazaki-Kariwa nuclear power station operated by Tokyo Electric Power Company.
Kashiwazaki-Kariwa, located about 220 km (136 miles) northwest of Tokyo, was among 54 reactors shut after the 2011 earthquake and tsunami crippled the Fukushima Daiichi plant in the worst nuclear disaster since Chernobyl, Reuters reported.
Shishir Priyadarshi, president of Chintan Research Foundation, in an article in The Hindustan Times on Tuesday, said India’s shift to nuclear power could be a viable strategy with the country’s electricity demand set to double over the next two decades.
India was simultaneously trying to sustain economic growth, achieve energy security and deep decarbonisation rather than just legal fine-tuning though the new laws on nuclear energy, he added.
Priyadarshi noted that other countries had similarly incorporated nuclear power into their energy mix, citing examples such as France which derived more than two-thirds of its electricity from nuclear power, while in the United States private operators dominated nuclear generation.
“For decades, nuclear energy in India remained almost entirely within the public sector. That model, while understandable in the programme’s early years, is no longer adequate for the scale and speed now required,” he said.
